Codex final message:
Implemented multi-recipient notification encryption in [hushline/crypto.py]([redacted-path] [hushline/routes/common.py]([redacted-path] [hushline/routes/profile.py]([redacted-path] and [hushline/routes/message.py]([redacted-path] `encrypt_message` and `encrypt_bytes` now accept one or many recipient keys, full-body notification fallback encrypts for the full enabled recipient set, and resend no longer reuses a single-recipient armored body when multiple recipients are enabled, falling back to the generic notification instead.

I updated coverage in [tests/test_crypto.py]([redacted-path] [tests/test_routes_common.py]([redacted-path] [tests/test_notifications.py]([redacted-path] [tests/test_resend_message.py]([redacted-path] and [tests/test_behavior_contracts.py]([redacted-path] for multi-recipient key selection, per-recipient SMTP delivery, profile full-body encryption across enabled recipients, resend multi-recipient encryption, and the armored resend fallback path.

I didn’t run `make lint` or `make test`; that’s left to the runner.

        user.email_include_message_content = True

        )

        assert pgp_message_sig in response.text, response.text

    @pytest.mark.usefixtures("_authenticated_user")
    @pytest.mark.usefixtures("_pgp_user")
    @patch("hushline.routes.profile.encrypt_message")
    @patch("hushline.routes.profile.do_send_email")
    def test_notifications_enabled_yes_content_yes_encrypted_body(
        mock_do_send_email: MagicMock,
        mock_encrypt_message: MagicMock,
        client: FlaskClient,
        user: User,
    ) -> None:
        # Enable email notifications, with no message content
        user.enable_email_notifications = True
        user.email_include_message_content = True
        user.email_encrypt_entire_body = True
        db.session.commit()
    
        encrypted_email_body = (
            "-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----\n\nfake encrypted body\n\n-----END PGP MESSAGE-----"
        )
    
        response = client.post(
            url_for("profile", username=user.primary_username.username),
            data={
                "encrypted_email_body": encrypted_email_body,
                "field_0": msg_contact_method,
                "field_1": msg_content,
                **get_profile_submission_data(client, user.primary_username.username),
            },
            follow_redirects=True,
        )
        assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
        assert "Message submitted successfully." in response.text
    
        message = db.session.scalars(
            db.select(Message).filter_by(username_id=user.primary_username.id)
        ).one()
        assert len(message.field_values) == 2
        for field_value in message.field_values:
            assert pgp_message_sig in field_value.value
    
        response = client.get(url_for("message", public_id=message.public_id), follow_redirects=True)
        assert response.status_code == 200
        assert pgp_message_sig in response.text, response.text

Codex final message:
Adjusted notification encryption target selection to preserve the legacy single-recipient path while keeping the new multi-recipient behavior. In [hushline/routes/common.py]([redacted-path] `notification_email_encryption_target()` now falls back to `user.pgp_key` when there are no enabled notification recipient rows, which restores the full-body encrypted notification behavior expected by the failing profile/message tests.

Added a narrow regression test in [tests/test_routes_common.py]([redacted-path] to lock that fallback in place alongside the existing multi-recipient coverage.

I did not run `make lint` or `make test`.

Codex final message:
Adjusted the notification encryption target assembly in [common.py]([redacted-path] so it includes the legacy `user.pgp_key` when a secondary enabled recipient exists but no primary recipient row has been materialized yet. That keeps the multi-recipient full-body path on server-side encryption instead of incorrectly treating the request as a single-recipient armored-body reuse case.

Added a focused regression in [test_routes_common.py]([redacted-path] to lock that legacy-primary-plus-secondary case in place.

Codex final message:
Adjusted [hushline/routes/common.py]([redacted-path] so `notification_email_encryption_target()` now identifies the real primary notification row by `position == 0` and excludes it from the secondary list by object identity, not DB ID.

That fixes the repeated failure mode in the new multi-recipient tests: unflushed recipients all have `id=None`, so the previous `recipient.id != primary_recipient.id` check dropped valid secondary recipients, and the helper also misclassified a lone `position=1` recipient as the primary row. I did not run `make lint` or `make test`.
